file_name: Columbia Laboratories, (Bermuda) Ltd. - AMEND NO. 2 TO MANUFACTURING AND SUPPLY AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: Fleet will at all times ensure that the Columbia Equipment meets and is operated and maintained in accordance with Applicable Laws and cGMP and GDP.
perturbation.changed_text: Fleet will endeavor to ensure that the Columbia Equipment meets and is operated and maintained in general alignment with Applicable Laws and cGMP and GDP.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires Fleet to 'ensure' compliance with all Applicable Laws, cGMP (current Good Manufacturing Practice), and GDP (Good Distribution Practice). The modified text weakens this obligation by using 'endeavor to ensure' and 'general alignment,' creating ambiguity about the level of compliance required, making it difficult to enforce.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR Part 211 - Current Good Manufacturing Practice Regulations
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 211.22 mandates written procedures for production and process control designed to assure that drug products have the identity, strength, quality, and purity they purport or are represented to possess. The modification allows for a weaker 'general alignment,' which does not guarantee adherence to these detailed and specific requirements, thus contradicting the assurance of quality mandated by the CGMP regulations.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act) | FDA Skip to main content Skip to FDA Search Skip to in this section menu Skip to footer links In this section : Laws Enforced by FDA Home Regulatory Information Laws Enforced by FDA Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act) Laws Enforced by FDA FD&C Act Reference Information United States Code, Title 21 The laws of the United States are organized by subject into the United States Code. The United States Code contains only the currently enacted statutory language. The official United States Code is maintained by the Office of the Law Revision Counsel in the United States House of Representatives. The Office of the Law Revision Counsel reviews enacted laws and determines where the statutory language should be codified related to its topic. The Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act and subsequent amending statutes are codified into Title 21 Chapter 9 of the United States Code. The listing of FD&C Act sections presented here identifies both the FD&C Act and U.S. Code section numbers, which can be used to narrow your search on the Law Revision Counsel website. To search the FD&C Act on the Law Revision Counsel website, use the Advanced Search to limit the your search to Title 21. To search the FD&C Act on the Law Revision Counsel website, you may either search by U.S. code section number or browse the Title 21 section listing . Content current as of: 03/29/2018 Back to Top
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces 'ensure' with 'endeavor to ensure' and 'in accordance with' with 'in general alignment with,' thus weakening the obligation to comply with Applicable Laws, cGMP, and GDP. According to the Law Explanation, 21 CFR § 211.22 mandates procedures to 'assure' drug products have the required identity, strength, quality, and purity. The modified contract language, allowing for 'general alignment,' contradicts the assurance of quality mandated by CGMP regulations, making it an Ambiguities Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Fleet shall provide reasonable notice to Columbia and shall consult with Columbia before Fleet makes any material change in any manufacturing process for the Product.
perturbation.changed_text: Fleet shall attempt to provide notice to Columbia and may consult with Columbia before Fleet makes what it considers to be a possibly significant change in the manufacturing process for the Product.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires Fleet to 'provide reasonable notice' and 'consult' before any material change. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using 'attempt to provide notice,' 'may consult,' and 'what it considers to be a possibly significant change.' This makes the obligation discretionary and the definition of 'significant' subjective, weakening Columbia's oversight.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR § 314.70 - Supplements and other changes to an approved NDA
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 314.70 requires that changes to the manufacturing process of an approved drug product must be reported to the FDA via a supplement to the NDA. This regulation requires that significant changes in manufacturing be reviewed and approved by the FDA. The modified text weakens the manufacturer's obligation to notify and consult, potentially leading to changes being made without proper regulatory oversight, thus risking violation of this FDA regulation.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens obligations, potentially leading to non-compliance with regulatory requirements. The original contract required 'reasonable notice' and 'consultation' before material changes, while the modified text uses 'attempt to provide notice,' 'may consult,' and 'what it considers to be a possibly significant change.' This makes the obligations discretionary, which contradicts the intent of 21 CFR § 314.70, which requires reporting significant changes to the FDA. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 21 CFR 314.70, supporting the law explanation. The alteration makes compliance uncertain and undermines regulatory oversight.

perturbation.original_text: Fleet shall keep the Columbia Equipment safe and in good working order and shall take all reasonable steps to ensure that the Columbia Equipment does not become contaminated or corroded.
perturbation.changed_text: Fleet should consider keeping the Columbia Equipment generally safe and in working order and might take steps as it sees fit to reduce the risk of the Columbia Equipment becoming contaminated or corroded.
perturbation.explanation: The original text uses 'shall keep' and 'shall take all reasonable steps,' imposing a clear obligation on Fleet. The modified text replaces these with 'should consider keeping' and 'might take steps as it sees fit,' making the obligation optional and subjective. This creates ambiguity regarding Fleet's responsibility for maintaining the equipment and preventing contamination.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 211.180 mandates that equipment used in the manufacturing, processing, packing, or holding of a drug product shall be of appropriate design, adequate size, and suitably located to facilitate operations for its intended use and for its cleaning and maintenance. The modified text introduces ambiguity, potentially leading to inadequate maintenance and an increased risk of contamination, thereby contradicting the strict requirements of the CGMP regulations. The phrase 'might take steps as it sees fit' does not ensure that appropriate measures are actually taken, as required by the regulation.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to maintain equipment appropriately, contradicting the requirement in 21 CFR § 211.180, as explained by the Law Explanation, because it replaces mandatory language ('shall keep', 'shall take all reasonable steps') with discretionary language ('should consider keeping', 'might take steps as it sees fit'). While the scraped snippets provided do not directly contain the text of 21 CFR § 211.180, they confirm its existence and relevance within the Code of Federal Regulations, supporting the argument that the changes in the contract create ambiguity and a potential failure to meet regulatory maintenance requirements. This ambiguity makes the obligation difficult to enforce.
